title: What Do We Do When Studies Disagree?
text:
good afternoon I'm Barry Kramer the
associate director for disease
prevention at the National Institutes of
Health I'm very pleased to be here this
afternoon to provide opening remarks for
the 16th year of the NIH Gordon lecture
this award is given each year to a
scientist who has made major research
contributions in the field of
epidemiology or clinical trials it is
presented by the National Institutes of
Health based upon recommendations from
senior epidemiologists at the
NIH the Gordon lecture was established
in 1995 and tribute to Dr Robert Gordon
for his dedication to the field of
epidemiology and his distinguished
service to the National Institutes of
Health during more than 30 years of
service to the NIH he served in numerous
senior leadership positions including
director of the clinical Center and
ultimately as special assistant to the
NIH
director his breath of knowledge and
experience in the clinical Sciences led
him to become Chief adviser for clinical
practice and research to two NIH
directors for the last 10 years of his
life Dr Gordon made important
contributions to inter Institute policy
and management issues regarding
epidemiology clinical trials and the
health effects of environmental hazards
this award lecture is named in his honor
in memory of his distinguished career
and unique scientific
contributions this year's Gordon lecture
award recipient is Dr Julie burring
she's currently professor of medicine at
Harvard Medical School and Brigham and
Williams hospital and professor of
epidemiology at the Harvard School of
Public Health Dr burring is an
internationally recognized leader in the
study of cardiovascular disease and
cancer her seminal work in
cardiovascular disease in women has made
Broad immeasurable contributions to the
field of women's health for many years
the study of primary and secondary
prevention of cardiovascular disease was
conducted nearly exclusively in men the
groundbreaking women's health study a
randomized controll trial of lowd dose
aspirin and vitamin E for the prevention
of cardiovascular disease as well as
cancer among 40,000 female health
professionals was conducted under Dr
buing Bing's
leadership as a result important
differences in Aspirin treatment among
women versus men were recognized for the
first time important Public Health
recommendations came from that study Dr
Bing's work also quantitated clinically
important adverse effects of aspirin
treatment that are important to informed
decision-making by women and their
Physicians a primary focus of Dr burring
research has been on the analytic and
clinical epidemiology of chronic
diseases she has been in an intellectual
leader in the design conduct analysis
and interpretation of several Landmark
large scale randomized clinical trials
and observational cohorts she is still
the principal investigator of the
women's health study which continues to
follow participants
observationally she is co-investigator
of the Physicians health study 2 which
evaluated the preventive effects of
vitamin E vitamin C and betacarotene on
cardiovascular disease cancer vision and
cognitive fun function among 15,000 male
Physicians she is current she currently
is co-investigator of the newly funded
randomized trial of vitamin E and fish
oil for the primary prevention of
cardiovascular disease and cancer known
as vital that will be initiated among
20,000 men and women this year Dr
burring has published extensively and
has served as a reviewer or editor on
numerous scientific peer- review
journals she believes strongly in giving
back to the scientific Enterprise by
serving as an active member of
professional societies and giving of her
time to serve on NIH study sections
advisory committees and daa safety
monitoring
boards in fact Dr burring is the chair
of the National Cancer institute's
prostate lung coloral and ovarian cancer
screening study or the plco study and it
has been my pleasure as an investigator
of the plco to work with her for more
than a decade I'm proud to claim Julie
as a colleague and as a
friend Dr burring has received numerous
Awards and honors most recently the
Roger L Nichols excellence and teaching
award from the Harvard School of Public
Health in 2007 and the Jon Snow award
from the American public health
association in
2008 among her students and peers Dr
burring is treasured as a skilled mentor
to many young promising investigators
and has been honored with numerous
awards for teaching over the years she's
actively involved in the teaching and
training of students and fellows in
epidemiology and is co-director of an
NIH t32 training Grant in the
epidemiology of cardiovascular disease
her dedication and enthusiasm are an
inspiration to many doctoral students
who have gone on to careers in both
research and clinical
medicine the topic of this afternoon's
presentation is of great interest to the
research
Community it's also a source of
confusion and sometimes frustration to
policy makers the general public and
journalists when they're faced with
differing results from what appear to be
similar studies today's presentation
will accentuate the point that we must
look at the totality of evidence in
order to understand where we are in the
knowledge of a particular research
question the talk will explore the
strengths and limitations inherent in
virtually every study design exploration
of discrepant results can lead to
improved study designs and a more
informed analysis of study
results I'm delighted to introduce Dr
Julie burring as this year's recipient
of the NIH Gordon lecture
award her presentation is entitled what
do we do when studies disagree she'll
speak for about 40 minutes and then the
audience will have an opportunity to ask
her questions for another 15 minutes
there'll also be a reception which in
this year is in the NIH Library around
the corner after the lecture to allow
more discussion with her and one-on-one
interactions please join me in giving Dr
burring a very warm welcome
jul good afternoon everyone um Dr Kramer
thank you so much for that very kind
introduction um it's really very nice to
be here it's very much a feeling of
coming home when I'm back here I'm
honored um to have gotten this award to
be giving the Robert S Gordon Jr lecture
in epidemiology to today but the reason
it me means so much is cuz it's coming
from my community at the NIH and I have
been very fortunate to be able to work
with many of you through my data safety
and monitoring board activities or by my
study section activities or through
workshops and as a result there are so
many of you here who truly are my
colleagues and my friends and it's just
very nice that that uh I would be
receiving this and be speaking with you
today the other thing that was nice when
I started to pull together my talk was
that it gave me the opportunity to just
think a little bit I was thinking about
discrepant results but I was trying to
put it in a broader context and the
context I was thinking about is sort of
what I have seen change over these last
few years and then I could think about
how the results were going to fit into
that and there was sort of two sections
of changes that I thought that I had
seen and the first one is that there
really has been a major change in the
Public's understanding about the
importance
the importance of epidemiology studies
and that they're actually seeking the
results of epidemiologic studies don't
forget I came from a time that when I
first got my degree I never told anybody
that I was an epidemiologist because
nobody actually knew what it was that I
did and they would be very proud if they
thought that meant I was a skin doctor
of some kind and after we explained what
epidemiology was I just said I was in
medical research but that's not a
problem anymore people are much more
aware of it people are much more
appreciative of it and as a corollary of
it there's an increasing focus on
everybody on the idea of prevention of
disease and how
epidemiology uh epidemiologic studies
clinical trials can add to that
information and why has that happened
well in part because this country is
aging and that's making a difference and
in particular if you just look at the
executive summary of The United Nations
report on world population aging there's
four major findings that are directly
related to it the population aging in
the world is unprecedented by 2050 the
number of older people in the world will
exceed the number of the young for the
first time in history the population
aging is pervasive it's all over the
world we we're dealing with this it's
profound it's having major complic uh
consequences and implications for all
facets of human life economic social
political and it's enduring it's not
going away at all
and so the change in Focus has been that
not only do we want to live longer do we
want to live at all and then live longer
but we want to do it in a good way we
want to reduce disability we want to
improve our quality of life we want to
do what's called successful aging so not
just getting older and not dying but
doing it successfully and we want our
morbidity to be compressed meaning that
we are healthy living the life that we
want to for a very long period of time
and then there's a very short transition
phase to become having morbid events and
then mortal events so prevention is
something that we believe is very
important or people believe is very
important not just treatment and it's
not just the United Nations that's
worrying about it the quote that I think
most of us feel as we get older was by
Ubie Blake who is a jazz pianist who
said if I had known I was going to live
this long I would have taken better care
of myself and the point that we try to
get across to everybody is that it
doesn't matter when you start yes it
would have been better if we did
everything well when we were in our
teens or in our early adulthood but that
even if we start in middle age or adult
or older age what we do makes a
tremendous difference to what happens to
us after
that so if we care about living well
aging
well and if prevention is important to
us then the next things are actually
corelates of it is that there's been a
substantial increase in the complexity
of the interpretation of a study's
findings we're not talking about Magic
Bullets anymore we're beyond the
penicillin type era we're really at the
point where we can come up with things
we are looking for things that are small
effect
statistically but very meaningful to us
from a clinical or a public health
standpoint
but if they're small then it's like
finding a needle and a Hast stack to
find them definitively to demonstrate
them
definitively and so what we have to do
is deal with that complexity both in
terms of our understanding the
implications of it for the results of
the studies we do and we read and also
in terms of communicating our study
findings so that we give accurate
messages and we do better
studies and why is that so important
because we truly have sort of a public
relations crisis out there in terms of
being an epidemiologist I'm sure all of
you have seen this slide but I really
remember it and I keep it because it
reminds me of what kind of questions
people ask me as much as I could go on
forever about the relative merits of
different kinds of bias and what it
means in terms of the validity of
certain kind of results you know that
the bottom line questions that people
want to know is just tell me is it
margarine or is it butter that I'm
supposed to be eating or the number one
question that I get asked is I know
exercise is good for me what is the
minimum amount of exercise that I need
to do to be able to have a benefit on
cardiovascular disease and cancer and
then when someone sees something they
don't like how many times have we said
well just let the scientists keep on
going they'll change their mind tomorrow
which is sort of the underlying of this
cartoon but it isn't just the public
that gets a little bit concerned
concerned about not understanding what's
happening and confused it's the
scientists too the ones who actually
should be able to read the literature
and there was a a poll that I thought
was very telling about 2000 attendees at
a Primary Care Medicine convention
Workshop CME kind of thing half of them
were Physicians and they just asked them
two Le two questions prior to the
lectures in particular since the
publication of the women 's Health
Initiative my understanding of the risks
and benefits of hormone replacement
therapy is and 48% of them said much
less clear than it was before and since
the publication of the findings my
comfort with counseling patients about
hormones and men and menopause is much
lower
54% and so this isn't a matter of not
being trained it's a matter of the
complexity of the kind of data that we
see and then finally a little picture
that I saw as I made my own personal
change from being a classically trained
epidemiologist to having to integrate
genetics into my studies biomarkers into
my studies there was a wonderful
Christian Science monitor cartoon of
someone in the front of a roller coaster
on the DNA Helix with a look on his face
that is exactly how I felt when I
realized that I had to figure out how
genetics would would go into and
complement the kind of studies that I
was doing and how was I going to analyze
it and how was I going to interpret
it so why is this so hard it's hard
because no one study can definitively
answer a research
question so we know that a research
question can be evaluated by a number of
different epidemiologic strategies that
we all pick them on the basis of the
question in terms of Logistics in terms
of our resources in terms of our
money and what we feel but need to
remember explicitly is assessing where
we are and our knowledge about any
question is going to require looking not
at one study but at a totality of
evidence and that totality of evidence
is going to include basic research
whether it be animals whether it be
genetics whether it be molecular biology
that gives us the underpinnings for the
mechanism by which our intervention or
risk factor or exposure may be working
and then the epidemiologic studies which
which are our direct evidence in humans
and they will come in many varieties and
the two biggest ones that we'll talk
about today are the observational
studies where we simply watch what
people choose to do and the intervention
studies the randomized clinical trials
where we try to make it as close to that
basic research as we can by trying to
control or intervene on the
environment well observational studies
it's often said that there's a hierarchy
to studies and the random clinical
trials are sort of the gold standard of
the research um I would say that more in
general I think every study has its
Niche and for every Niche every question
that we're trying to answer there is a
study that is sort of the not
necessarily the very best you can do but
the appropriate one to start with so
when I think about observational studies
there's no question that they have
advantages in terms of being simpler
being less expensive than doing a
randomized trials but that's more of a
logistics issue not a science issue when
I want to go to the observational
studies it's when I'm looking at the big
effects which usually is not what I do
when the required duration of exposure
is long and that's a very important one
because trials are usually on the order
of 3 to 5 years and when something may
to take longer to show us a effect on a
chronic condition observational studies
really can give us information that
other other kind of designs may not be
able
to sometimes it's the only way to go
meaning that it's not feasible or
ethical to do a trial we'll talk about
that in a second it's always going to be
useful it's always going to be extremely
useful for us to go the next step and to
design a new trial because through our
metaanalysis and through our systematic
reviews it is the way to look at these
observational studies and get a sense of
where we are in our knowledge as of
right now what order of magnitude effect
are we talking about and then the most
important thing is they work there are a
tremendous number of risk factors and um
um preconditions to getting an outcome
that we have learned through our
observational studies of course starting
with cigarette smoking since you can't
randomize something that's harmful so
when we look at it we may say that we
want to go to our next step to trials
but we must must never underestimate how
much observational studies do for us but
there are limitations of observational
studies that are inherent meaning it
isn't that if you did a better study
trained better learned under a master of
doing observational studies they would
be different this is inherent in the
design and in particular it's because
the exposure is self- selected and any
time people select an exposure they
select more than that there is a whole
host of EXP exposures or Lifestyles that
go along with eating a certain way
acting a certain way getting a certain
kind of drug and so there is a risk of
selection bias or differences in
outcomes that are not due to the
treatment itself but to these other
factors that are related to the exposure
and if we understood them well we would
actually be in better shape with respect
to observational studies but we know so
little about so many diseases in terms
of risk factors so the adjustment made
may not be adequate for these
confounders because we don't exactly
know what the risk factors are or in
fact if we don't know about them we
can't even even measure them adequately
to be able to do this so really what
observational studies can do is give us
the best Al be at a little vague answer
to real life questions while we always
know we're going to be saddled with
concerns and residual doubts about
confounding so the randomized clinical
trial how does that complement
observational studies well yes it's more
costly yes it's more logistically
difficult yes there's issues of ethics
and feasibility that you won't see in an
observational study we know you can't
randomize harm we know that people have
to be willing to be randomized in a
study rather than just being followed
but if we can do them if they are welld
designed and conducted their Niche is
that we because they are minimizing bias
and confounding because of the way they
are designed the randomized double blind
Placebo controlled intention to treat
analysis those aren't Ivory Tower words
they are all to minimize bias and
confounding and because of that they can
accomplish in their design if they're
welld designed and conducted what the
observational studies strive to do and
have difficulty doing they can detect
statistically small to moderate effects
which are so important to us but so hard
to definitively evaluate because of
their
design now so if we said that
observational studies gave us this best
vague answer to real life questions
trials will give us a precise answer by
minimizing confounding but to very
specific questions which may not in fact
reflect real life so how do those go
together well it has been shown that in
a majority of situations ations or many
situations can that can be found that
the trials and the observational studies
give us about the same order of
magnitude and there was a article in the
New England Journal that documented that
there was a commentary on afterward that
said we found little evidence that
estimates of treatment effects are
consistently larger or qualitatively
different than those that you get in the
trials but the fact remains there are
situations where in fact they are
different and it's when they are
different that we learn so much about
sharpening our skills intoing analysis
and absolutely sharpening our skills
about designing better studies for the
future and so what do we do what do what
could it be when they're different well
it could be that something went wrong
with the observational study it could be
that something went wrong design-wise
with the trial could be that both of
them were not designed well and they
just came to different answers or it
actually could be that both of them did
answer the question that they were given
to answer but they're answering
different questions so what have we
learned from that that will allow us to
do better in the
future so I want to use very quickly
four examples that I've sort of lived
through over this last period of time
hormone therapy risks and benefits from
the Women's Health Initiative aspirin
and cardiovascular disease in men versus
women in the women's health study
beta carotene and lung cancer or
nutrient so beta carotene and lung
cancer and then vitamin E and
cardiovascular disease and I'm going to
touch on these not so we go over the
findings because you know these examples
but we go over what then the message was
that needed to be looked at to figure
out what happened the Women's Health
Initiative we all know very well
observational study plus a trial and
part of the trial was to look at hormone
replacement therapy on heart disease
osteoporosis and breast cancer women who
did not have a uterus were given
conjugated estrogen plus meth um plus
progesterone versus placebo as opposed
to women who had had a
hysterectomy and the point was to look
at whether um the estrogen plus
progestin decreased heart disease and
osteoporosis related fractures increased
risk of breast cancer so that basically
how did it come out on a balance of
risks and benefits on overall
health now remember where we started
when we started the trial the trial had
to be in a context a totality of
evidence and the totality of evidence
was about as consistent on observational
studies as we could see that based on
more than 40 observational studies if
you looked at the summary relative risk
the summary relative risk was a 36%
reduction in risk of coronary heart
disease among women who had ever used
hormone replacement therapy and about a
50% reduction among those who were never
users and they were consistent whether
you did population-based cohorts
cross-sectional case control studies it
didn't matter you were seeing the same
kind of finding and many people felt
that it was so consistent that it was
actually unethical to give a woman
Placebo because they would be she would
be denied the benefits of hormones but
what swayed the thinking to allowing
enough investigators to do the trial is
confounding that women who took hormones
for an extended period of time were
going to be different than women who
didn't take hormones for other reasons
that could in themselves affect the
outcome under study if they were doing
this to prevent their risk or lower
their risk of heart disease they were
probably leaner they were probably not
smoking as much they were more
physically active they saw their doctors
more regularly they were more educated
they were sort of doing everything that
they could and this was one of their
weapons they were using to reduce risk
of heart disease but all these other
factors could in themselves affect the
risk of heart disease that we were
looking at so the only way to get around
it to get those confounders evenly
divided was to do a trial randomly
assign them to hormone use or Placebo
follow them up and if we still saw a
difference then it was likely to be due
to the hormone and not the fact
not to the differences among these other
factors well the data and safety
monitoring board watched the trial as it
should and after 5 years of data they
decided that the estrogen and progestin
group needed to stop their study pills
because of unexpected findings
everything we're going to talk about is
always unexpected this is not anything
that anybody thought was going to happen
yet we needed to examine it and learn
from it that the balance of harm and
benefit was was not at all what we
thought it was going to be the hormone
I'm sorry the lipids were being
benefited just like they should lowering
total cholesterol lowering LDL raising
HDL but the clinical end points weren't
being benefited by The Taking of
hormones and in effect what the findings
were is completely unexpectedly there
was an increased risk of coronary heart
disease increased risk of stroke of
pulmonary embolism of breast canc cancer
decrease in hip fracture decrease in
coloral cancer so the equipoise that the
trial had started with was no longer
present in the dsnb acted on that they
also though calculated just because you
have a relative increase what does that
do for your absolute risk so they worked
out for thousand 10,000 person years on
the drug that there would be 19
additional Adverse Events per 10,000
person years but the important important
thing is how low that was so even though
it was more in the active group and it
increased it by 50% or 100% the absolute
risk was low and that's very important
so first we had to give a message to
people and the message is that estrogen
plus progestin shouldn't be initiated or
continued if you're doing it just for
the primary prevention of coronary heart
disease and if you're doing it for the
primary prevention of osteop porosis
then given the other risks it would be
better to weigh them against the
benefits and see if there's other
available agents and given the low
absolute risk and the relationship of
the risk to time after menopause it can
still have a clinical role for people
who are taking them for menopausal
symptoms using the lowest effective dose
for the shortest period of time so that
was sort of dealing with the
participants in the public but what
about us what was the message to
researchers well the message to
researchers was basically what happened
here how is it possible that not only
did it not benefit heart disease but it
went in the other direction so let's
look at it a little more closely and go
through the steps that were examined the
first is that they looked at all the
outcomes for this study CHD stroke
pulmonary embolism hip fractures breast
cancer and color rectal cancer and if
you notice the observational versus is
the trial were right in line for
absolutely everything except coronary
heart disease so now the question is not
how is it possible that the
observational study showed a benefit and
we actually showed harm but it was for
coronary heart disease that that
happened and not for something else
that's sort of like if you think of NCIS
or Law and Order or something like that
we're getting our little Clues to go a
little bit farther and try to figure out
what the problem really is well it could
of course course be methodologic
differences it could be the confounding
that was uncontrolled in the
observational study it could be that the
compliers are different than
non-compliers I understand all this and
all of that challenged us to develop
better analytic methods to try to be
looking at a subgroup of the trial and a
subgroup of the observational study that
was most alike in terms of these factors
it also raised a caveat that I don't
think any of us had actually thought of
before that if you're in an
observational study where you send out a

possible for an early effect that your
Baseline you didn't use the drug you
didn't use replacement hormones you then
started to take it you had a problem you
came off of it and by the end of the by
the two years when you next got a
questionnaire you were a non-user again
so you come up as a non-user at Baseline
non-user at two years and what you miss
completely is in fact you did take the
drug in the middle something happened
associated with The Taking of the drug
and that was not going to be picked up
by this kind of studies that was a real
learning experience for a lot of people
with um observational
studies but the real difference was
biology so if I'm trying to repeat what
happened in an observational study how
close was I to the observational study
and in part it was because the
characteristics were different
these were older women they were 63 10
years after menopause as opposed to the

menopause they didn't have vasom motor
symptoms in the in the Whi but they did
in the observational study they were
heavier their endogenous hormones were
different is what we're trying to say
rather than just exogenous hormones so
that maybe and this is when we then go
back to the biologists now we're back to

epidemiology back to the basic sciences
that maybe in fact the progression of
cardiovascular disease as it goes along
over time goes through a period right
after menopause whereas a favorable
lipid and endothelial effects of
estrogen predominate and you'll have a
favorable influence of initiating estro
exogenous estrogens but as you continue
on over time you go to another period in
a woman's life where there are
prothrombotic and pro-inflammatory
effects of estrogen predominating and we
could have an adverse effect and this
was something the subtlety was something
that was not known before but was known
from seeing the data and taking it back
to the basic scientists and saying help
us work out what could possibly be
happening so the results were sure the
method methodologic limitations led to
an overestimate of the benefits of
hormones but we can we can work on that
we can try to control control better the
elevation of risk and coronary heart
disease might never have been seen
otherwise the biological difference ones
make it that these are two questions
that are not directly comparable they're
really just addressing different
questions it led us for the community to
better analytic approaches of comparing
data from the two kinds of studies and
it also led to a subsequent study to
evaluate which was now an unanswered
question which is what happens if you
start women on estrogen within 3 years
of their last menstrual period maybe
that is closer to the observational
study and that is a trial called keeps
which is currently
ongoing so let's think about another
kind of example women's health study
randomized double blind Placebo
controlled trial of lotos aspirin and
vitamin E in the prevention of
cardiovascular disease and cancer
conducted among
39,821 female health professionals
funded by National Cancer Institute
National heart lung and Blood Institute
and the pills were provided by industry
the trial is now over we're following
the women observationally so this was
our randomization a quarter of them got
aspirin a quarter of them got B um
vitamin E quarter of them got both a
quarter of them got neither 2 by two
factorial design when we started aspirin
I would have placed money on knowing the
results of this trial we know in
secondary preven among those who have
had evidence of cardio clinical evidence
of cardiovascular disease aspirin
reduces your risk of having a second
event or dying from cardiovascular
disease in both men and women and we
know if you're in the middle of having
an acute coronary event that if you give
someone an aspirin crushed or chewed you
will lower their risk of having a second
event dying from that event during the
high-risk period in both men and women
primary prevention healthy people would
it help us to take an aspirin every day
to prevent an event that may or may not
occur sometime in the future well um we
knew in men that it reduce the risk of a
first heart attack but there weren't
much data on women and we felt that if
you're going to make a recommendation
for half the population you should have
some data on that part of the population
but I really would have put money down
that you would have seen a benefit on
myocardial infarction so the
recommendations from aha from the US
preventive Services Task Force really
were for men and women but the direct
evidence was sparse and that was the
reason for starting the aspirin and
cardiovascular component I just want to
show you a couple pictures just for a
second you know I'm talking a lot about
doing these trials like that you know
you just go out and you get 40,000 women
and you do them women were not used to
being in clinical trials of heart
disease heart disease trials had
traditionally been of middle-aged men
because that was where the highest in
Ence of heart disease was so we really
had to do a lot of Education to teach
them about randomization about not
knowing what pill you on aspirin or
Placebo vitamin E or not and getting
them to understand that we could design
the nicest trial and be fortunate enough
